retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Ophthalmic Arteries -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: Among clinically relevant, practical anatomy, important anomalies pertinent to the ophthalmic artery warrant being mentioned at this juncture. Dilenge and Ascherl (1980) reported on 42 anomalies of the ophthalmic and middle meningeal arteries. In that study, approximately 2.4% of anomalies involve the separation of ocular branches of ophthalmic artery from extraocular intraorbital branches. The same figure (2.4%) has been noted for the presence of accessory ophthalmic artery. In approximately 7.1% of cases, the ophthalmic artery arises from the middle meningeal artery. Approximately 17% of anomalies involve the ophthalmic artery arising from an extradural portion of the carotid siphon. The most common anomaly of this vascular circuit (about 40% of cases) is the middle meningeal artery arising from ophthalmic artery. [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Ophthalmic Arteries -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: As mentioned previously, the ophthalmic artery continues medially as the superior and inferior muscular branches. These important branch vessels originate either directly from the ophthalmic artery or from a separate trunk that subsequently divides into the superior and inferior branches. These branches provide blood supply to the extraocular muscles.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Ophthalmic Arteries -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: In summary, the ophthalmic artery can arise from the middle meningeal artery, middle cerebral artery, or the posterior communicating artery. In addition, the middle meningeal artery can arise from the ophthalmic artery. Furthermore, the cavernous origin of the ophthalmic artery has been reported. Finally, the presence of persistent stapedial artery has been well described by neurovascular anatomy experts. All of the above anatomic variations must be recognized at the time of invasive procedures involving related blood supply to this critically important body region. For example, embolization of a tumor supplied by the external carotid artery may result in loss of vision if the eye depends on the anatomically anomalous routing of blood to maintain retinal perfusion.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Eye Arteries -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The branches of the ophthalmic artery comprise the entire arterial supply to the eye. Most commonly the ophthalmic artery branches off of the internal carotid artery, distal to the cavernous sinus, then travels through the optic canal. The ophthalmic artery has multiple branches which separate into two categories: orbital branches and optical branches. The orbital arteries include the ciliary arteries, central retinal artery, and muscular arteries. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Eye Arteries -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Anterior Ciliary Arteries: There are seven anterior ciliary arteries that branch from the muscular arteries and run with the extraocular muscles. The anterior ciliary arteries supply the rectus muscles, conjunctiva, and sclera before joining the long posterior ciliary arteries to form the major arterial circle of the iris. [2] Each rectus muscle receives its vascular supply from two anterior ciliary arteries, except the lateral rectus which receives blood supply from only one anterior ciliary artery.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Eye Arteries -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: Ciliary muscles and the six extrinsic eye muscles receive vascular supply by branches of the ophthalmic artery. Long posterior ciliary arteries provide blood to the ciliary muscles. The superior, lateral, medial and inferior rectus muscles receive blood from anterior ciliary arteries. Each rectus muscle receives supply from two anterior ciliary arteries, except the lateral rectus which receives blood from only one artery. The lateral muscular artery also supplies the lateral and superior rectus as well as the superior oblique muscle. The medial muscular branch supplies the inferior and medial rectus and inferior oblique muscles.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Ophthalmic Arteries -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The ophthalmic artery gives off several posterior ciliary arteries that pass through the sclera and supply the posterior uveal tract. Because the posterior ciliary vessels are end vessels, sudden occlusion can produce infarction in the region of the choroid.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Eye Nerves -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics -- Optic Nerve
retrieved_docs.content: Intracranial Anterior cerebral artery Anterior communicating artery Internal carotid artery Superior hypophyseal artery Anterior choroidal artery Posterior cerebral artery Anterior choroidal artery Calcarine artery Posterior temporal artery Parieto-occipital arteries Middle cerebral artery Occipital artery Basilar artery Posterior communicating artery Extracranial Central retinal artery Ophthalmic artery Long and Short posterior ciliary arteries

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Eye Muscles -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The majority of the blood supply to the orbit is supplied by the ophthalmic artery, which branches off of the internal carotid artery. A branch of the external carotid artery, the infraorbital artery, also contributes blood supply to the orbital floor. There are many branches of the ophthalmic artery, including the central retinal, supraorbital, supratrochlear, lacrimal, dorsal nasal, short posterior ciliary, long posterior ciliary, posterior ethmoidal, anterior ethmoidal, and anterior ciliary (off of the muscular branches of the ophthalmic artery) arteries. Except for the central retinal artery and the ciliary arteries, which supply intraocular structures, these branches, as well as the infra-orbital artery off of the external carotid, all contribute to the vascular supply of the extraocular muscles and structures. The superior and inferior ophthalmic veins are responsible for venous drainage of the orbit.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Eye Arteries -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Central Retinal Artery : It is the first branch of the ophthalmic artery. It is a terminal branch supplying the inner layer of the retina, and its occlusion can cause sudden visual loss. It travels inferiorly and within the optic nerve sheath to supply the inner two-thirds of the retina. The artery further divides into superior and inferior arcades, which form the blood-retina barrier.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Eye Arteries -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Muscular branches : The two muscular branches of the ophthalmic artery that supply extraocular muscles include the medial and lateral muscular branches. The medial artery being larger than the lateral muscular branch.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Eye -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: All eye structures, except for the eyelids and conjunctiva, are supplied by branches of the internal carotid artery. The external carotid artery supplies the eyelids and conjunctiva. The internal carotid artery has many branches; one is the ophthalmic artery. This artery branches into the posterior ciliary, retinal, and muscular arteries. These arteries supply blood to the ciliary bodies, retina, and muscles, respectively. The central retinal artery is a branch of ophthalmic artery which runs within the optic nerve.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Ophthalmic Arteries -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Other branches of the ophthalmic artery include the ethmoid arteries, medial palpebral vessels, and terminal branches.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Ophthalmic Arteries -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: Despite tremendous accumulated knowledge of the vascular anatomy, the understanding of the arterial development in the human embryo from the branchial to the postbranchial stage is still relatively poor. Key considerations in the area of blood supply to the orbit include the presence of both internal and external carotid contributions, with this dual "circuit" arrangement structured on embryological origins. In the majority of cases, communication between the middle meningeal artery and the ophthalmic artery is present, passing through the superior orbital fissure in most cases. Less commonly, an additional meningo-orbital foramen (foramen of Hyrtl) may be present lateral to the superior orbital fissure, with the connecting branch known as the sphenoidal artery, recurrent meningeal artery, or orbital branch of the middle meningeal branch.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Eye Arteries -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Long Posterior Ciliary Arteries: The long posterior ciliary arteries (1 to 2) travel near the optic nerve and pierce the posterior sclera to supply the choroid and ciliary muscle before joining the major arterial circle of the iris. [2] The major arterial circle of the iris distributes branches to the iris and ciliary body.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Ophthalmic Arteries -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Medial Palpebral Arteries = The ophthalmic artery courses anteriorly toward the trochlea. Here, the superior and inferior medial palpebral arteries originate. This is the blood supply to the eyelids.

retrieved_docs.content: Moreover, diagnostic observations and pathological findings related to the choroidea are specifically localized to the site termed “حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي,” indicating a genetically related placental structure. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2021
retrieved_docs.content: The category “إجراءات هيستوباثولوجيا، غير مصنف في موضع آخر” (histopathology procedures not classified elsewhere) functions as a broad parent classification for specialized medical procedures. Within this hierarchy, “diagnostic procedures on eye” are identified as a child of this parent, indicating that eye‑focused diagnostic methods are grouped under the umbrella of unclassified histopathology procedures. Likewise, “التشريح” (anatomical or dissection procedures) is also listed as a child of the same parent category, showing that both eye diagnostic procedures and anatomical/dissection procedures share the same higher‑level classification. This structured relationship clarifies how specific diagnostic and anatomical interventions are organized within broader histopathology categories, supporting accurate documentation, coding, and coordinated patient care.

Additional specific sub‑conditions listed under the same category are انفصال مشيمية العين النزفي, تنكس حصوي, and حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي. Together, these entities illustrate the extensive and heterogeneous nature of choroidal and retinal disorders, underscoring the importance of precise classification for effective patient care, targeted research, and accurate medical coding.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 1231
retrieved_docs.content: The retina serves as the primary anatomic site for a variety of ocular pathologies, including retinal detachments, structural defects, vascular occlusions, and retinopathy. In patients with secondary diabetes mellitus, both non‑proliferative retinopathy and moderate non‑proliferative retinopathy are identified as retinal findings, underscoring that diabetic retinopathy manifests within the retinal tissue. Additionally, the condition referred to as داء ريفسام also presents its findings in the retina. Recognizing the retina as the common location for these disorders is essential for accurate diagnosis, monitoring, and management in ophthalmic and systemic disease care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: detachment of the eye (انفصال مشيمية العين) and hemorrhagic choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين النزفي). Peripheral involvement includes peripheral disseminated choroiditis with chorioretinitis and peripheral focal retinitis with retinochoroiditis. Across all entities, the choroidea (choroid) is the anatomical locus of the characteristic lesions, underscoring its central role in pathophysiology, clinical assessment, precise diagnosis, and tailored management of choroidal and retinal disorders.

Understanding these interlinked concepts supports integrated patient assessment and multidisciplinary care planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2476
retrieved_docs.content: The retina is the anatomical site where a variety of ocular pathologies are observed, including retinal detachments, structural defects, vascular occlusion, and retinopathy. Both moderate nonproliferative retinopathy due to secondary diabetes mellitus and non‑proliferative retinopathy due to secondary diabetes mellitus are identified in the retina, highlighting its role as the primary finding site for diabetic‑related retinal disease. In addition, the condition referred to in Arabic as داء ريفسام also manifests in the retina. Recognizing the retina as the common location for these diverse findings supports focused clinical assessment and appropriate management of retinal disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2261
retrieved_docs.content: The retina serves as the anatomical site for a range of ocular pathologies, including retinal detachments, structural defects, vascular occlusion, and various forms of retinopathy. Both mild nonproliferative retinopathy due to secondary diabetes mellitus and non‑proliferative retinopathy due to secondary diabetes mellitus are identified on the retina, indicating that diabetic retinal changes are localized to this tissue. Additionally, the condition referred to in Arabic as داء ريفسام also has the retina as its finding site, further emphasizing the retina’s central role in ocular disease manifestation. Recognizing that these diverse abnormalities share the retina as their common location aids clinicians in targeted examination and management of retinal disorders.

retrieved_docs.content: Facilitation of intraocular circulation is linked to زَرَق, indicating that زَرَق may be involved in promoting or supporting blood flow within the eye. This relationship suggests that زَرَق could have therapeutic relevance for conditions where ocular perfusion is compromised, such as glaucoma or retinal vascular disorders, and highlights the importance of understanding how زَرَق influences intraocular vascular dynamics in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 228
retrieved_docs.content: without complication, with a further branch “secondary diabetes mellitus, without mention of complication” and a specialized form “secondary diabetes mellitus with diabetic peripheral angiopathy with gangrene.” Patients with secondary diabetes mellitus are predisposed to multiple microvascular complications. Mild, moderate, and general non‑proliferative retinopathy attributed to secondary diabetes mellitus, as well as broader retinopathy and nephropathy linked to the same disease, all arise from the underlying secondary diabetes mellitus and have the retina as the primary finding site. The retina also serves as the anatomical site for retinal detachments, retinal defects, vascular occlusions, and various other forms of retinopathy. In addition, the retina functions as a finding site for داء ريفسام, indicating that ocular examination of the retina can reveal manifestations of this disease and underscoring the importance of retinal assessment for accurate diagnosis and management of

retrieved_docs.content: intracapsular extraction of the lens, one‑stage intracapsular extraction of the lens by the inferior temporal route with insertion of a prosthetic intraocular lens, and other lens‑removal actions (إخراج العدسة). These procedures have direct procedure sites in the ocular lens, the lens capsule, and are performed in the eye (oči). Facilitation of intraocular circulation is classified under the term زَرَق, which is the parent category for glaucoma. Glaucoma (member of الزَرَق, SMQ) is linked to specific surgical interventions such as استئصال التربيق and بضع الزاوية, both belonging to the الزَرَق grouping and to operative procedures on the anterior segment of the eye, with direct anatomical relationships to the eye (oči).
